title: Fatigue and pallor with heavy periods

chart.oneLiner: 38F presents with months of progressive fatigue, exertional breathlessness, and pallor, on a background of heavy menstrual periods.

opening: I'm exhausted all the time and I get winded going up the stairs — it's not like me.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 38-year-old woman, tired-appearing, otherwise well. Good historian.

HPI: Months of fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, exertional dyspnea, and occasional lightheadedness. On asking: heavy, prolonged menstrual periods for over a year (changes pads hourly on heavy days). Craves and chews ice (pica) — only if asked. NO black/bloody stools, NO weight loss, NO abdominal pain.
PMH: none. MEDS: none (no NSAIDs). SOCIAL: vegetarian-leaning diet; no alcohol excess.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, comfortable.
- Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 96, RR 16, 36.8°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- HEENT: conjunctival pallor; smooth tongue if examined; koilonychia (spoon nails) if examined.
- Cardiac: regular, soft flow murmur, no other findings. Abdomen: soft, nontender. Rectal: brown stool, no melena (if examined).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the menorrhagia, pica, or diet unless asked about menstrual history, cravings, or diet.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: Hgb 8.9, MCV 72 (low — microcytic), MCH low, RDW high, WBC normal, Plt 480 (reactive thrombocytosis).
labs.Smear: Microcytic, hypochromic red cells with anisocytosis; pencil cells.
labs.IronStudies: Ferritin 6 (low), serum iron low, TIBC HIGH, transferrin saturation low — iron deficiency pattern.
labs.Reticulocyte: Low/inappropriately normal for the degree of anemia.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia ~150 with sawtooth flutter waves, best seen in II/III/aVF; ~2:1 conduction.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia ~150 with sawtooth flutter waves, best seen in II/III/aVF; ~2:1 conduction. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial flutter with sawtooth waves

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5) on a PA film; lungs otherwise clear.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5) on a PA film; lungs otherwise clear. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this anemia (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Iron deficiency anemia (leading,8). Anemia of chronic disease (core,3). Thalassemia trait (core,3). Occult GI blood loss / malignancy (CANT-MISS to consider source,4). Sideroblastic anemia (bonus,2). Names iron deficiency and the need to identify the SOURCE — here menorrhagia (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Iron deficiency anemia. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Occult GI blood loss / malignancy. Also reasonable: Anemia of chronic disease; Thalassemia trait.

coreActions.item: Names iron deficiency and the need to identify the SOURCE — here menorrhagia

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC with indices (CANT-MISS,4). Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC (CANT-MISS,5). Reticulocyte count (2). Peripheral smear (2). Identify source — menstrual history; in higher-risk/older patients, GI evaluation (2). PENALTY: transfusing a stable, chronically anemic patient unnecessarily (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC with indices; Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC.

penalties.item: transfusing a stable, chronically anemic patient unnecessarily

contraindicatedTests: transfusing a stable, chronically anemic patient unnecessarily

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); no acute ischemic changes (compensatory tachycardia from anemia) (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
criticalFindings: Atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction (ventricular rate ~150).
commonMistakes: mislabeling as sinus tachycardia at a rate of 150
missing the flutter waves in the inferior leads
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction; classic sawtooth waves in the inferior leads, ventricular rate ~150.

criticalActions: Atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction (ventricular rate ~150).

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes low yield (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies cardiomegaly on a PA film
commonMistakes: calling a normal heart enlarged on an AP/portable film
steps.idealAnswer: Cardiomegaly with a cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on an adequate PA film.

coreActions: identifies cardiomegaly on a PA film

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the microcytic indices and low ferritin/high TIBC, what is your diagnosis and the likely source?
steps.rubric: Iron deficiency anemia (6). Cites low ferritin + high TIBC + microcytosis, with menorrhagia as the source (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH anemia work-up: iron repletion – oral ferrous sulfate or IV iron (CANT-MISS,6); identify and treat source of blood loss – GI work-up / menstrual history (CANT-MISS,6); confirm iron studies (low ferritin) (3); transfuse only if symptomatic/unstable (3); recheck CBC/retic response (2). PENALTY: transfusing a stable iron-deficient patient without indication (-2); failing to pursue the bleeding source (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Replace iron – oral ferrous sulfate (every-other-day dosing improves absorption) or IV iron if intolerant/malabsorption/ongoing loss/need for rapid repletion. Critically: identify and treat the source of blood loss – GI evaluation (EGD/colonoscopy) in adults, menstrual history in women. Transfuse only if symptomatic/unstable. Recheck CBC/retic.

criticalActions.item: Identify and treat source of blood loss

requiredActions: Identify and treat source of blood loss

Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 38F presents with months of progressive fatigue, exertional breathlessness, and pallor, on a background of heavy menstrual periods. The diagnosis is Iron deficiency anemia, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular, soft flow murmur, no other findings. Abdomen: soft, nontender. Rectal: brown stool, no melena (if examined).
HEENT: conjunctival pallor; smooth tongue if examined; koilonychia (spoon nails) if examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Iron deficiency anemia while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Iron deficiency anemia and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
managementPearls: Identify and treat source of blood loss
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia (compensatory); no acute ischemic changes.
criticalManagementItems: Identify and treat source of blood loss
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction (ventricular rate ~150).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction (ventricular rate ~150).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
